coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their application in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Liping
Song, Xinhong
Chen, Yiying
Rong, Mingcong
Zhao, Tingting
Wang, Yiru
Jiang, Yaqi
Chen, Xi
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, the highly intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is revealed. This activity was greatly dependent on pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration. The experimental results showed that the stable N-GQDs could be used for the detection of H2O2 and glucose over a wide range of pH and temperature, offering a simple, highly selective and sensitive approach for their colorimetric sensing. The linearity between the analyte concentration and absorption ranged from 20 to 1170μM for H2O2 and 25 to 375μM for glucose with a detection limit of 5.3μM for H2O2 and 16μM for glucose. This assay was also successfully applied to the detection of glucose concentrations in diluted serum and fruit juice samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity
Colorimetric detection
H2O2 and glucose sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of N-GQDs
The effects of different pH values, temperatures and H2O2 concentrations on the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs
The stability and kinetic analysis of the N-GQDs
Determination of H2O2 and glucose using the N-GQDs peroxidase-like catalytic reaction
Selectivity and analysis of practical samples based on the proposed assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the colorimetric detection of glucose by combining the glucose oxidase (GOx) catalytic reaction with the N-GQDs peroxidase-like catalytic reaction.

(a) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm for the detection of H2O2 using the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs. (Inset: typical photographs of the colorimetric detection for H2O2 in 200μL 100mM H3PO4–NaH2PO4 buffer solution (pH 3.0) at 35°C. From left to right: (1) 200μL 50mM H2O2 and 200μL 5mM TMB, colorless; (2) 500μL 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs, bright yellow; (3) 500μL 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs, 200μL 5mM TMB and 200μL 50mM H2O2, turning blue.) (b) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652nm in the presence of different concentrations of N-GQDs in pH 3.0H3PO4–NaH2PO4 buffer solution at 35°C. (For interpretation of the references to color in the text and the legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs and catalytic activity of HRP against pH (a), temperature (b) and H2O2 concentration (c). (Inset: the catalytic activity of HRP against H2O2 concentration.) Experiments were carried out using 500μL 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs or 500μL 4ngmL−1 HRP in a reaction volume of 3.0mL, different amounts of H2O2, with 5mM TMB as substrate at 35°C with 100μL 100mM H3PO4–NaH2PO4 buffer solution (pH 3.0) for N-GQDs or 100μL 100mM NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer solution (pH 4.0) for HRP, unless otherwise stated. The maximum point in each curve was set as 100%. Each point is an average of three successive measurements. Error bars represent the standard error derived from the three successive measurements.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) linear calibration plots for H2O2 detection using N-GQDs peroxidase-like catalytic reaction; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra and (d) linear calibration plots for glucose detection using GOx and N-GQDs. (Inset: dose-response curves for H2O2 detection using N-GQDs peroxidase-like catalytic reaction (a) and glucose detection using GOx and N-GQDs (c); photographs for colorimetric detection for H2O2 (b) and glucose (d), respectively.) Experimental conditions for H2O2 and glucose detection: 500μL 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs, 200μL 5mM TMB, 5μL 40mgmL−1 GOx and different volumes of 50mM H2O2 or 1mM glucose in 200μL 100mM H3PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer solution (pH 3.0) at 35°C. Each point is an average of three successive measurements. Error bars represent the standard error derived from three successive measurements.

Selectivity of glucose detection based on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs. The change of absorbance at 652nm was measured after the addition of N-GQDs for 30min, and the change of absorbance for glucose was normalized to 100%. The concentrations were 0.75mM for glucose and 3.75mM for the other four sugars. Inset: typical photograph of glucose detection with the colorimetric method using GOx and N-GQDs as the catalysts (from left to right: (1) blank; (2) glucose; (3) fructose; (4) maltose; (5) sucrose; (6) lactose). The picture was taken 1h after the addition of N-GQDs. Error bars represent the standard error derived from three successive measurements.
Comparison of the apparent Michaelis–Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max) between N-GQDs and HRP.

head.ce:title: Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their application in the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Science, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Marine Environmental Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China. Tel.: +86 592 2184530; fax: +86 592 2184530.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, the highly intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is revealed. This activity was greatly dependent on pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration. The experimental results showed that the stable N-GQDs could be used for the detection of H2O2 and glucose over a wide range of pH and temperature, offering a simple, highly selective and sensitive approach for their colorimetric sensing. The linearity between the analyte concentration and absorption ranged from 20 to 1170μM for H2O2 and 25 to 375μM for glucose with a detection limit of 5.3μM for H2O2 and 16μM for glucose. This assay was also successfully applied to the detection of glucose concentrations in diluted serum and fruit juice samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their immense catalytic efficiency, strong substrate specificity and good stereoselectivity under mild reaction conditions, natural enzymes not only participate in nearly all cellular metabolic processes in nature, but also have been widely applied in pharmaceutical production, chemical industry, food processing and agriculture [1–3]. Nonetheless, some serious deficiencies of natural enzymes greatly impede their widespread applications, such as the harsh preparation and storage conditions, tedious purification process, high cost, poor stability, volatile structure and easy deactivation induced by physicochemical factors. To circumvent the above problems, it is highly desirable to develop potential alternatives such as natural enzyme mimetics and artificial enzymes. Consequently, the development of artificial enzymes has very rapidly emerged as a lively field of research. Up to now, a variety of nanomaterials, such as metal oxide-based nanomaterials [4–7], graphene oxide (GO) [8,9], carbon nanotubes [10–12], carbon nanodots [13–16], carbon nitride sheets [17,18] and dots [19], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [20–22], noble metal-based nanomaterials [23–25] and so on, are shown to possess unique intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activities. These nanomaterials are suitable for bioanalysis owing to their small size, excellent stability, and biocompatibility. However, compared with carbon nanomaterial-based peroxidase mimics, the catalytic reactions of metal oxide-based nanomaterials and noble metal-based nanomaterials are generally carried out in an acidic solution, which seriously restricts their applications in biological systems.

Recently, luminescent GQDs, one type of zero dimensional graphene sheets with lateral size less than 100nm, have attracted researchers because of their robust biological and chemical inertness, tunable luminescence emission, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, and resistance to photobleaching [26,27]. Meanwhile, both theoretical study and experimental results have proved that heteroatom doped GQDs can offer more active sites and effectively modulate their intrinsic physicochemical properties including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features, resulting in many new performances and applications [28,29]. Up to now, the synthesis, properties and advanced applications of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) have become headline-grabbing studies, and N-GQDs with favorable fluorescence properties for bioimaging [30,31] and electrocatalytic activity for light-emitting diodes [32] are reported.

N-GQDs, with a large surface area ratio and more active sites, may serve as promising candidates as peroxidase mimics, with the advantages of excellent water-solubility, resistance against stringent conditions, low cost and tunability in catalytic activities. In this work, dopamine was selected as a nitrogen source owing to its biocompatibility and self-polymerization capability in alkaline conditions under an oxygen atmosphere. N-GQDs were synthesized through strong acid oxidation of three dimensional N-doped graphene aerogel (3D NGA). We discovered that the produced N-GQDs presented intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the oxidation between H2O2 and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), resulting in a blue colored product. Since H2O2 is a main product in the glucose oxidase (GOx) catalytic reaction, combining the N-GQD and GOx catalytic reactions, we developed a simple, highly selective and sensitive colorimetric assay for the detection of H2O2 and glucose (Scheme 1 ), which was successfully applied to the detection of glucose concentrations in diluted serum and fruit juice samples.

Synthetic graphite powder was purchased from Lvyin Co. (Xiamen, China); dopamine was obtained from Alfa Aesar; potassium permanganate, concentrated sulfuric acid, sodium nitrate, fructose, lactose, sucrose, maltose and glucose were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); TMB was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Product of Switzerland); horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and GOx were purchased from Life Science Products & Services; and 30% H2O2 was from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tec Co., Ltd. HRP and GOx were biological reagents, and the other reagents were of analytical grade. They were all used as received without further purification. Cellulose membrane dialysis tubing (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); serum samples were obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Xiamen University (Xiamen); apple juice, orange juice and peach juice samples were purchased from local supermarkets (Xiamen, China); and pure water (18.2MΩ), obtained from a Millipore Autopure WR600A system (Millipore Ltd., USA), was used throughout the experiment.

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on a UV2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission microscope observations were performed on a TECNAI F-30 electron microscope (Philips-FEI, Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The high resolution transmission microscopy (HRTEM) observations were performed on a TECNAI F-30 electron microscope (Philips-FEI, Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system, Physical Electronics, USA) was used to investigate the functional groups on the N-GQDs surface with all the binding energies being calibrated with C 1s as reference energy (EC1s =284.6eV).

N-GQDs were prepared by treating 3D NGA as reported in our previous work [33] with strong acid. Typically, GO was first synthesized with natural graphite powder, sodium nitrate, potassium permanganate and concentrated sulfuric acid according to Hummers’ method [34]. The 3D NGA was obtained via a hydrothermal approach using dopamine as the nitrogen source. Then, 8.0mg 3D NGA was treated with 6mL concentrated H2SO4 and 2mL concentrated HNO3 at 75°C for 6h. After cooling to room temperature, 1M NaOH solution was added into the acidified N-GQD solution to adjust the pH to 1, and then Na2CO3 power was added to neutralize the pH to 7.0. Finally, the solution was subjected to dialysis to completely remove salt ions in a dialysis bag overnight to obtain the N-GQDs. The stock N-GQDs solution was obtained by concentrating the N-GQDs to 25mL.

Kinetic measurements were carried out in a time course mode by monitoring the absorbance change at 652nm on a UV2550 spectrophotometer. Experiments were carried out in a 3mL reaction solution at 35°C. 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs, 5mM TMB, 50mM H2O2 and 100mM H3PO4–Na2H2PO4 buffer solution (pH 3.0) were used throughout the experiments. The Michaelis–Menten constant was calculated using a Lineweaver–Burk plot: 1/v 0 = K m/V max C +1/V max, where v 0 is the initial velocity, V max is the maximal reaction velocity, and C is the concentration of the substrate [7,9].

In this detection, 1mL reaction solution containing 5μL 40mgmL−1 GOx, 100μL glucose of different concentrations and 200μL 10mM NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer solution (pH 5.5) was incubated at 35°C for 1.5h. Then, 500μL 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs, 200μL 5mM TMB and 200μL 100mM H3PO4–NaH2PO4 buffer (pH 3.0) were added into 300μL of each of the above reaction solutions. Finally, after being diluted to 3mL with water, the mixture was incubated at 35°C and used to perform the time course measurement.

Based on the previous reports [9,15], the detection of glucose in serum and fruit juice samples was carried out as follows: the serum samples were treated by ultrafiltration on a 10kDa ultrafiltration membrane and centrifugation at 104 rpm for 40min. Each fruit juice sample (apple, orange and peach juice) was centrifugated at 104 rpm for 40min, and the supernatant was diluted 20-fold [9,16]. 100μL of the serum supernatant and each of the individual diluted fruit juices were added into a solution containing 5μL 40mgmL−1 GOx and 200μL 10mM NaH2PO4–Na2HPO4 buffer (pH 5.5). Then, each mixture was diluted to 1mL using water and kept at 35°C for 1.5h. Subsequently, 500μL 320μgmL−1 N-GQDs, 200μL 5mM TMB and 200μL 100mM H3PO4–NaH2PO4 buffer (pH 3.0) were added into 300μL of the above reaction solutions. Finally, after being diluted to 3mL with water, the mixtures were incubated at 35°C and used to perform the time course measurement. Comparison of the glucose concentrations obtained using the above method and those provided by the hospital was carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, the N-GQDs displayed peroxidase-like catalytic activity in the oxidation of TMB. In the presence of the N-GQDs, the solution containing H2O2 and TMB changed to a blue color, while there was no significant color change in the TMB+H2O2 or TMB+N-GQDs solution. We noticed that there was no metal catalyst in the N-GQDs from the XPS spectrum as shown in Fig. S1. These results indicated that the N-GQDs possessed peroxidase-like catalytic activity, and that the N-GQDs, TMB and H2O2 are all necessary for the color change. In addition, the profile of the time-dependent absorbance change at 652nm (Fig. 1b) indicated that the reaction rate and absorbance change greatly increased with the increase of the N-GQDs concentration.

There are two possible reasons for the above observations concerning the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs. On the one hand, previous study reports that the sp2 hybridized carbon structure and the large surface area ratio endow nanomaterials with peroxidase-like catalytic activity [35]. The high crystallinity of N-GQDs with a lattice parameter of 0.24nm (Fig. S2) indicated that there was a diffraction plane (1120) of sp2 graphitic carbon in the N-GQDs. Additionally, a density functional theoretical study demonstrates that the peripheral carboxylic/carbonyl moieties on the surface of graphene are crucial to the reduction reaction of H2O2 [36]. On the other hand, the introduction of N atoms owning five valence electrons into benzene ring atoms can efficiently influence the spin density and the charge distribution of the carbon atoms, enhancing the density of the catalytically active centers on the graphene surface with low stereohindrance for binding redox species in catalytic reactions [29]. The UV–vis spectra comparison between GQDs and N-GQDs in the catalytic oxidation of TMB further confirmed the effect of nitrogen-doping (Fig. S3). Meanwhile, the donation of the lone-pair of electrons in the amino groups of TMB being absorbed on the N-GQDs surface can also confer an increase in electron density and mobility in the N-GQDs [11]. As a result, the electron transfers from the top of the valence band of the N-GQDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of H2O2, and the catalytic oxidation of TMB by H2O2 are carried out in the presence of the N-GQDs with peroxidase-like catalytic activity [9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of different pH values, temperatures and H2O2 concentrations on the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to HRP, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs was dependent on pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration. In our work, the catalytic activity of the N-GQDs was evaluated at different pH values ranging from 1.0 to 14.0, and temperatures from 25 to 65°C. The control experiments using HRP as catalyst were carried out under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 2 , the optimal pH and temperature were 3.0 and 35°C for the N-GQDs and 4.0 and 35°C for the HRP. However, the N-GQDs exhibited high activity over a broad pH range. The catalytic activity of the N-GQDs remained 85.7% at pH 6.0 compared to that at pH 3.0. In contrast, the catalytic activity of HRP at pH 6.0 was only 28.7% of that at pH 4.0 (Fig. 2a). Moreover, the catalytic activity of HRP was nearly inhibited above 60°C, while the catalytic activity of the N-GQDs was still about 21% of that at 35°C when the temperature was above 60°C (Fig. 2b). In addition, in order to obtain the maximum catalytic activity for N-GQDs, over a 1000 fold higher H2O2 concentration than that for HRP was used (Fig. 2c), indicating that the N-GQDs possessed more catalytic activity than HRP at higher H2O2 concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and kinetic analysis of the N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare the catalytic activity of the N-GQDs and HRP, the steady-state kinetic parameters were determined for the reaction using H2O2 and TMB as substrates under optimal conditions for the N-GQDs and HRP. Although different H2O2 concentrations were necessary for the catalytic reaction of HRP and the N-GQDs (Fig. 2c), the typical Michaelis–Menten curves were observed for the N-GQDs (Fig. S4a and b) and HRP (Fig. S4c and d) within a suitable range of substrate concentrations. The kinetic data were obtained by varying one substrate concentration and fixing the others. The initial reaction rate was calculated (Table S1) and applied to the double reciprocal of the Michaelis–Menten equation. The apparent steady-state kinetic parameters K m and V max were obtained from a Lineweaver–Burk plot and are shown in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, using H2O2 as the substrate, the K m value of the N-GQDs was 61.1 fold larger than that of the HRP, indicating that the N-GQDs required a higher H2O2 concentration to obtain maximal activity. In addition, we found that the K m value of the N-GQDs in TMB was 22.9 fold less than that of HRP-TMB, which revealed that the N-GQDs presented a higher affinity to TMB. The catalytic activity of the N-GQDs was measured over a range of TMB and H2O2 concentrations (Fig. S4e and f). The double-reciprocal plots, at a fixed concentration of one substrate versus different concentrations of the other substrate for H2O2 or TMB, presented the characteristic parallel lines of a ping-pong mechanism, which was similar to that observed for HRP [9,14]. These results implied that the N-GQDs adsorbed and reacted with the first substrate, then released the first product before reacting with the second substrate, which was similar to HRP and some other nanomaterials [3,9].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 and glucose using the N-GQDs peroxidase-like catalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the color variation of TMB oxidation affected by the change of H2O2 concentration with the N-GQDs, a colorimetric and quantitative detection of H2O2 could be developed based on the relationship between the H2O2 concentration and the absorbance intensity at 652nm (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3b, the absorbance change at 652nm showed a good linear relationship with the H2O2 concentration ranging from 20 to 1170μM with a detection limit of 5.3μM H2O2. The linear regression equation was A =0.0038+0.056 C H2O2 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9943. Simultaneously, the color variation of the solution was obvious and easily observed by the naked-eye (Fig. 3b inset). Since H2O2 is the main product of the GOx-catalyzed reaction, the N-GQDs were also applied in the colorimetric and quantitative detection of glucose instead of HRP (Fig. 3c). As noted in Fig. 3d, the absorbance at 652nm was proportional to the glucose concentration in the range 25–375μM with the detection limit of 16μM glucose under optimal conditions. The linear regression equation was A =0.0044+0.1232 C glucose with a correlation coefficient of 0.9909. The color variation for the glucose response was also obvious by visual observation (Fig. 3d inset).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and analysis of practical samples based on the proposed assay
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity approach for glucose was evaluated under the co-existence of fructose, maltose, sucrose and lactose. The results (Fig. 4 ) showed that there was an obvious color change for the solution containing 0.75mM glucose (Fig. 4 inset). For the solutions containing 3.75mM fructose, maltose, sucrose or lactose, no obvious absorption or color could be observed (Fig. 4), which could be attributed to the high affinity of GOx for glucose. These results confirmed that the proposed assay had good selectivity toward glucose. The proposed assay was applied to the detection of glucose in diluted serum samples (Table S2) with the glucose concentrations in the serum samples being 11.74, 13.91 and 5.65mM and three commercial fruit juices (Fig. S5). Given the fact that the glucose concentrations in the serum samples were in the general range of diabetic (9–40mM) and healthy persons (3–8mM) [37], the results obtained using our method (Table S2) were close to those provided by the hospital, indicating the applicability of this colorimetric method for glucose sensing in serum samples. Moreover, the comparison of the glucose concentration obtained by the proposed method and glucose meter (Acon Bitech (China, Hangzhou) Co., Ltd.) (Table S3) also suggesting the reliability of the proposed method in practical application.

In summary, we demonstrated that the N-GQDs possessed a peroxidase-like catalytic activity. Similar to HRP, the catalytic activity of the N-GQDs was dependent on pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration. The kinetic analysis indicated that the N-GQDs possessed excellent catalytic activity. As a novel natural enzyme mimetic, the N-GQDs displayed several advantages over natural enzymes, such as low-cost, good stability, high surface area ratio and high affinity to substrates through π–π interactions. Furthermore, using the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs, a simple, highly selective and sensitive colorimetric and quantitative assay for H2O2 and glucose was proposed. The assay was applied to the detection of glucose in diluted serum and fruit juice samples. The experimental results revealed that the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the N-GQDs could be expected to facilitate the application of this method in medical diagnostics and to biosamples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Nature Scientific Foundation of China (No. 21375112), Program of Science and Technology of Xiamen for University Innovation (No. 3502Z20143025), Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (PCSIRT, No. IRT13036) and NFFTBS (No. J1310024), which are gratefully acknowledged. We thank Professor John Hodgkiss of The University of Hong Kong for assistance with the English in the paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.02.024.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytically active nanomaterials: a promising candidate for artificial enzymes
Engineered enzymes for improved organic synthesis
Incorporating graphene oxide and gold nanoclusters: a synergistic catalyst with surprisingly high peroxidase-like activity over a broad pH range and its application for cancer cell detection
Oxidase-like activity of polymer-coated cerium oxide nanoparticles
The artificial peroxidase activity of magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles and its application to glucose detection
Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetics and their applications in H2O2 and glucose detection
Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles
Colorimetric platform for visual detection of cancer biomarker based on intrinsic peroxidase activity of graphene oxide
Graphene oxide: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application to glucose detection
Visual and quantitative detection of copper ions using magnetic silica nanoparticles clicked on multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Label-free colorimetric detection of single nucleotide polymorphism by using single-walled carbon nanotube intrinsic peroxidase-like activity
A glucose biosensor based on direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase immobilized on nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes
B-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Low-cost preparation of photoluminescent carbon nanodots and application as peroxidase mimetics in colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Multicolor luminescent Carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, supramolecular assembly with porphyrin, intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity and applications
Graphite-like carbon nitrides as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
Ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets: a low-cost, green, and highly efficient electrocatalyst toward the reduction of hydrogen peroxide and its glucose biosensing application

A general strategy for the production of photoluminescent carbon nitride dots from organic amines and their application as novel peroxidase-like catalysts for colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Highly-efficient peroxidase-like catalytic activity of graphene dots for biosensing
In situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
Graphene quantum dots-band-aids used for wound disinfection
A dual fluorometric and colorimetric sensor for dopamine based on BSA-stabilized Au nanoclusters
Colorimetric and ultra-sensitive fluorescence resonance energy transfer determination of H2O2 and glucose by multi-functional Au nanoclusters
Caged-protein-confined bimetallic structural assemblies with mimetic peroxidase activity
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Luminescent graphene quantum dots as new fluorescent materials for environmental and biological applications
Electrocatalytic activity of nitrogen-doped carbon nanotube cups
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
One-step preparation of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots from oxidized debris of graphene oxide
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Mussel-inspired, ultralight, multifunctional 3D nitrogen-doped graphene aerogel
Functional single-wall carbon nanotube nanohybrids–associating SWNTs with water-soluble enzyme model systems
The origin of the catalysis of hydrogen peroxide reduction by functionalized graphene surfaces: a density functional theory study
Noninvasive continuous monitoring of physiological glucose using a monosaccharide-sensing contact lens
